title: Hybrid MIL-Autoencoder Framework for Enhanced Anomaly Detection in Whale Vocalizations
abstract: The accurate detection and temporal localization of anomalies in whale vocalizations remain challenging due to high false normal rates and diverse acoustic environments. This research proposes a novel hybrid framework that integrates Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) with a Neural Network Autoencoder (NN-AE) to address these challenges. The proposed model leverages the strengths of MIL for handling weakly supervised learning scenarios and the NN-AE's capabilities in anomaly detection, thereby enhancing the precision and reliability of acoustic anomaly detection systems. The study will involve the development of a hybrid model architecture, which will be trained on comprehensive whale vocalization datasets and evaluated against a variety of marine soundscapes. Comparative analysis with existing models will be conducted to demonstrate improvements in anomaly detection accuracy and temporal localization. Additionally, the model will undergo hyperparameter optimization to facilitate real-time application in diverse marine environments. By addressing the limitations of high false normal rates and poor temporal localization, this research aims to contribute significantly to the fields of marine biology and acoustic monitoring. The anticipated outcomes include improved anomaly detection methods, enhanced real-time tracking capabilities, and broader applicability of AI and machine learning techniques in marine studies.
contributions: Development of a novel hybrid MIL-Autoencoder model
Improvement in anomaly detection accuracy and temporal localization
Advancement of AI techniques for real-time marine acoustic monitoring
